html:
Victor David Sjöström, born September 20, 1879, in Högerud, Silbodal parish, Värmland, and died January 3, 1960, in the Hedvig Eleonora parish of Stockholm, was a Swedish actor, director, and screenwriter. Alongside Mauritz Stiller, Sjöström became one of the leading figures in Swedish silent film. Among his many famous films are the adaptations of Selma Lagerlöf's works, *Ingmarssönerna* (1919) and *Körkarlen* (1921). From 1923 to 1930, Sjöström worked as a director at Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer studios in Hollywood, after which his work primarily focused on acting. As an actor, Sjöström is remembered for his final film role, as the aging doctor Isak Borg in Ingmar Bergman's *Smultronstället* (1957).

== Biography ==

=== Childhood and Adolescence ===

Victor Sjöström was born in 1879 in Silbodal, Värmland, as the third child of merchant Olof Adolf Sjöström and his wife, Elisabeth, née Hartman. His mother had previously been a traveling actress and was the sister of Victor Hartman, a leading actor at the Royal Dramatic Theatre (Dramaten) in Stockholm. His father was a businessman who had experienced business failures during the 1870s. When Victor Sjöström was born, the family lived in a simple fisherman's cottage by Lake Västra Silen. In 1880, the family emigrated to Brooklyn, New York, USA, where his father soon established a successful shipping agency.

In 1886, his mother died of postpartum fever, and his father remarried with the family's nanny, Maria Lovisa Olsson. Victor Sjöström had a difficult relationship with his stepmother, often arguing with her and even attempting to run away at one point. Consequently, in January 1893, his father sent him back to Sweden and Uppsala, where he lived with his aunt, the widow Næslund. He attended school in Uppsala and spent his school holidays with his actor uncle, who often took him to the Dramaten. As a teenager, he developed a strong interest in theatre and formed a theatrical company at the grammar school in Uppsala, which staged plays such as "The Two Mutes," where Sjöström reportedly showed an aptitude for comedy.

In 1895, Olof Sjöström returned from the USA. He had become poor again, and Victor Sjöström dropped out of school and moved in with him in Stockholm. He then began working to support himself and his father, including jobs at a lumberyard in Stockholm and as a seller of doughnuts on the city streets. In 1896, his father died, and Sjöström decided to seriously pursue an acting career.

=== The Life of a Touring Actor === Sjöström couldn't afford to attend the drama school at Dramaten, so instead he joined Ernst Ahlbom's theater company, with whom he toured in Finland from 1896 to 1898. Subsequently, he was employed by various theater companies: from 1898 to 1899 he worked at the Swedish Theatre in Helsinki, from 1899 to 1900 he toured with the Gothenburg People's Theater, and from 1900 to 1901 he was with Emil von der Osten. He married Alexandra Stjagoff, an actress born in Russia, on June 26, 1900; they separated around 1906 and divorced in 1912. From 1901 to 1902 he toured again with the Gothenburg People's Theater, from 1902 to 1904 and from 1907 to 1908 with Hjalmar Selander, from 1904 to 1905 and from 1906 to 1907 with Hugo Rönnblad's company, from 1905 to 1906 with Knut Lindroth, and from 1908 to 1909 with Axel Hultman. Between 1909 and 1911 he was the leader of Albert Ranft's regional tour, and subsequently he led Einar Fröberg's company from 1911 to 1912. From 1912 to 1913, he and Fröberg had a theater company together – the Fröberg-Sjöström Company – which performed guest appearances in places like Copenhagen.

=== The Early Films ===

In February 1912, Charles Magnusson, the head of Svenska Biografteatern, hired Sjöström for a film project, and that same year, he directed his first films. It was there that Sjöström met the future master director Mauritz Stiller, as well as the photographer brothers Henrik and Julius Jaenzon, who would later work with both Sjöström and Stiller. Sjöström made his acting debut in Stiller's film "The Vampire" to learn how a film shoot works. The film was shot in 1912 but wasn't released until 1913. Shortly after, he made his directorial debut with "The Gardener" (1912), where he not only directed but also acted alongside Gösta Ekman and Lili Bech, who would later become his second wife.

During his first five years in film, Sjöström directed a total of 30 films; among these early works, the masterpiece considered to be "Ingeborg Holm" (1913), which is notable for its social realism for the time. The film tells the story of a woman (played by Hilda Borgström) who, after her husband's death, loses custody of her children and ends up in a mental institution. The film garnered significant attention both for its artistic merits and for the debate about poverty relief that it sparked, and it also achieved success abroad.

=== The Golden Age ===

Sjöström's major breakthrough came in 1917 with his adaptation of Henrik Ibsen's "Terje Vigen," which is often considered the beginning of Sweden's golden age of film. It was Svenska Bio's most expensive film to date and was among the first films shot on location, specifically at Landsort near Nynäshamn. The film, based on Ibsen's long poem "Terje Vigen," tells the story of...

How certain are we about this?

Information about people from the 20th century is extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns facts but rather interpretation and evaluation, which can change over time and depending on the observer.
tldr: Victor Sjöström was a highly renowned Swedish actor, director, and screenwriter who became one of the most important figures in early Swedish silent film.
lattlast:
Victor Sjöström was born in 1879 in Värmland. He grew up in a family with strong ties to the theater and began working as an actor seriously after his father's death. Before he started making films, he toured extensively with various theatrical groups in Sweden and abroad.

In 1912, he began working in film and quickly became one of the country's leading directors and actors. He is known for having made many important films, including those based on stories by Selma Lagerlöf. He also worked in Hollywood for several years between 1923 and 1930.

Sjöström remained a well-known actor until his death in 1960. His final film role was as an elderly doctor in the film "Wild Strawberries," directed by Ingmar Bergman.
